112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}

118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."

136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.

151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. 205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. 230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238

240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.

242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244

247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.

250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md`. 
304	
305	**CRITICAL COST RULE**: Your report must be extremely concise. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and avoid conversational filler. Maximum ~100 words total. The plan agent does not need prose; it needs facts.
306	
307	```markdown
308	# Blueprint Writer Report: <slug>
309	**Status:** <COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
310	
311	## Changes
312	- Add `def:foo` (`Foo.bar`): captures XYZ.
313	- Revise `thm:bar`: updated `\uses{}`.
314	- Ref `references/hartshorne.md`: cited smooth criterion.
315	
316	## Notes / Strategy
317	- <ONLY if blocking or critical issue found. Keep to 1 sentence. Omit section if none.>
318	```
319	
320	## Return Value
321	
322	Your final assistant message:
323	
324	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
325	- The path to your full report.
326	
327	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.

124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.

126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.

136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.

140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}

151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}
178	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}

243	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the

310	\label{sec:grquot_chart}

384	\leanok
385	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
386	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
388	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
389	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
390	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
391	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
392	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
393	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
394	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
395	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
396	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
397	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
398	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
399	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
400	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
401	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
402	  \[
403	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
404	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
405	  \]
406	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
407	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
408	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
409	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
410	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
411	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
412	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
413	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
414	
415	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
416	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
417	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
418	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
419	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
420	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
421	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
422	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
423	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
424	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
425	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
426	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
427	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
428	  free sheaves'' primitive.
429	\end{definition}
430	
431	\begin{lemma}\leanok
432	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
433	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
434	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
435	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
436	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
437	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
438	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
439	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
440	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
441	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
442	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
443	  identity:
444	  \[
445	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
446	  \]
447	\end{lemma}
448	
449	\begin{proof}
450	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
451	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
452	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
453	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
454	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
455	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
456	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
457	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
458	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
459	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
460	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
461	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
462	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
463	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
464	\end{proof}

466	\begin{lemma}\leanok
467	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
468	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
470	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
471	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
472	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
473	\end{lemma}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
477	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
478	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
479	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
480	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
481	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
482	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
483	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
484	  \[
485	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
486	  \]
487	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
488	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
489	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
490	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
491	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
492	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
493	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
494	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
495	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
496	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
497	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
498	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
499	\end{proof}

503	\leanok
504	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
505	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
507	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
508	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
509	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
510	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
511	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
512	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
513	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
514	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
515	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
516	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
517	  %  by putting
518	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
519	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
520	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
521	  \[
522	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
523	  \]
524	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
525	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
526	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
527	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
528	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
529	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
530	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
531	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
532	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
533	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
534	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
535	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
536	\end{definition}

539	
540	\leanok
541	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
542	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
544	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
545	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
546	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
547	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
548	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
549	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
550	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
551	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
552	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
553	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
554	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
555	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
556	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
557	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
558	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
559	  \[
560	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
561	  \]
562	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
563	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
564	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
565	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
566	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
567	  \[
568	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
569	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
570	  \]
571	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
572	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
573	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
574	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
575	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
576	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
577	\end{definition}

579	\section{The functor of points}
580	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
581	
582	\begin{definition}
583	
584	\leanok
585	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
586	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
587	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
588	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
589	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
590	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
591	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
592	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
593	  % "represents the
594	  %  contravariant functor
595	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
596	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
597	  %  set of all equivalence classes
598	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
599	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
600	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
601	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
602	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
603	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
604	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
605	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
606	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
607	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
608	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
609	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
610	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
611	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
612	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
613	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
614	  \[
615	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
616	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
617	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
618	  \]
619	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
620	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
621	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
622	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
623	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
624	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
625	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
626	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
627	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
628	\end{definition}
629	
630	\section{The universal property}
631	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
632	
633	\begin{theorem}
634	
635	\leanok
636	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
637	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
638	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
639	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
640	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
641	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
642	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
643	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
644	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
645	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
646	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
647	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
648	  %  contravariant functor
649	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
650	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
651	  %  set of all equivalence classes
652	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
653	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
654	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
655	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
656	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
657	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
658	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
659	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
660	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
661	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
662	  \[
663	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
664	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
665	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
666	    \qquad
667	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
668	      \;\longmapsto\;
669	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
670	  \]
671	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
672	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
673	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
674	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
675	  universal family.
676	\end{theorem}
677	
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
680	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
681	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
682	    def:grassmannian_functor}
683	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
684	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
685	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
686	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
687	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
688	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
689	
690	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
691	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
692	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
693	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
694	  the composite
695	  \[
696	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
697	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
698	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
699	  \]
700	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
701	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
702	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
703	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
704	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
705	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
706	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
707	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
708	
709	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
710	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
711	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
712	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
713	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
714	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
715	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
716	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
717	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
718	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
719	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
720	
721	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
722	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
723	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
724	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
725	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
726	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
727	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
728	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
729	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
730	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
731	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
732	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
733	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
734	
735	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
736	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
737	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
738	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and

273	/-! ### Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary scheme morphism
274	
275	The functor of points (`AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor`) acts on morphisms by
276	pullback, and for that one needs `f^* (O^n) ≅ O^n` for an *arbitrary* scheme morphism
277	`f`. Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` supplies this only when the underlying
278	site functor `Opens.map f.base` is `Final`; the previous chapters discharged that `Final`
279	hypothesis only for open immersions and isomorphisms. The first lemma below removes the
280	restriction entirely: `Opens.map f.base` is `Final` for *every* scheme morphism, because
281	the structured-arrow category over any open `V` has a terminal object (the whole space
282	`⊤`). With that in hand, `pullbackFreeIso` and `pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank` hold for all
283	morphisms. -/
284	
285	/-- For an arbitrary scheme morphism `φ`, the site functor `Opens.map φ.base` is `Final`:
286	over any open `V` of the target the structured-arrow category `{U : V ≤ φ⁻¹ U}` has the
287	terminal object `U = ⊤`, hence is connected. This is the missing ingredient that makes
288	`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit`/`pullbackObjFreeIso` applicable to *every* morphism,
289	not just to open immersions. Project-local. -/
290	lemma opensMap_final {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) :
291	    (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base).Final := by
292	  constructor
293	  intro V
294	  set top : StructuredArrow V (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base) :=
295	    StructuredArrow.mk (Y := (⊤ : T.Opens)) (homOfLE le_top)
296	  haveI : Nonempty (StructuredArrow V (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base)) := ⟨top⟩
297	  apply zigzag_isConnected
298	  intro s t
299	  have hs : s ⟶ top := StructuredArrow.homMk (homOfLE le_top) (Subsingleton.elim _ _)
300	  have ht : t ⟶ top := StructuredArrow.homMk (homOfLE le_top) (Subsingleton.elim _ _)
301	  exact Relation.ReflTransGen.trans
302	    (Relation.ReflTransGen.single (Or.inl ⟨hs⟩))
303	    (Relation.ReflTransGen.single (Or.inr ⟨ht⟩))
304	
305	/-- **Pullback of a free sheaf of modules is free, for any scheme morphism**: for
306	`φ : T' ⟶ T` and an index type `I`, `φ^*(O_T^{⊕I}) ≅ O_{T'}^{⊕I}`. Built from
307	`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` once `opensMap_final` supplies the `Final` instance.
308	Project-local. -/
309	noncomputable def pullbackFreeIso {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) (I : Type u) :
310	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj (SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) I)
311	      ≅ SheafOfModules.free (R := T'.ringCatSheaf) I := by
312	  haveI := opensMap_final φ
313	  exact SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso φ.toRingCatSheafHom I
314	
315	/-- **Pullback preserves rank-`d` local freeness.** If `M` is locally free of rank `d` on
316	`T`, then `φ^* M` is locally free of rank `d` on `T'`, for any scheme morphism `φ`. The
317	chart cover `{U i}` of `T` trivialising `M` pulls back to the cover `{φ⁻¹ U i}` of `T'`;
318	on each member the restriction of `φ^* M` is identified with the pulled-back chart-free
319	sheaf via the pseudofunctor comparison `pullbackComp`, the factorisation
320	`φ ∘ (φ⁻¹ U i).ι = (φ ∣_ U i) ≫ (U i).ι` (`morphismRestrict_ι`), and `pullbackFreeIso`.
321	Project-local. -/
322	lemma pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) {M : T.Modules}
323	    {d : ℕ} (h : SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank M d) :
324	    SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank ((Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj M) d := by
325	  obtain ⟨ι, U, hcover, hloc⟩ := h
326	  refine ⟨ι, fun i => φ ⁻¹ᵁ (U i), Scheme.Hom.iSup_preimage_eq_top φ hcover, ?_⟩
327	  intro i
328	  obtain ⟨e⟩ := hloc i
329	  exact ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (φ ⁻¹ᵁ (U i)).ι φ).app M ≪≫
330	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (morphismRestrict_ι φ (U i)).symm).app M ≪≫
331	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (φ ∣_ (U i)) (U i).ι).app M).symm ≪≫
332	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback (φ ∣_ (U i))).mapIso e ≪≫
333	    pullbackFreeIso (φ ∣_ (U i)) (ULift.{u} (Fin d))⟩
334	
335	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules

358	/-! ## The functor of points and the universal property
359	
360	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme `T` to the set of *equivalence classes* of
361	rank-`d` quotients `q : O_T^r ↠ F` with `F` locally free of rank `d`. We encode a single
362	such quotient as the structure `RankQuotient r d T`, the equivalence as `RankQuotient.Rel`
363	(an isomorphism of the targets commuting with the quotient maps), and the functor's value
364	at `T` as the quotient `Quotient (rqSetoid r d T)`. The pullback action `rqPullback`
365	together with `pullbackFreeIso`/`pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank` (and the fact that the
366	pullback functor preserves epimorphisms, being a left adjoint) realise functoriality.
367	
368	Because a sheaf of modules `F : T.Modules` is a large object, this quotient lives in
369	`Type 1` (not `Type 0` as the original scaffold signature stated); the corrected universe
370	is the only change to the pinned signature, and it is forced — the substantive content is
371	unchanged. -/
372	
373	/-- A **rank-`d` quotient of `O_T^r`** on a scheme `T`: a sheaf of modules `F` on `T`,
374	locally free of rank `d`, together with an epimorphism `q : O_T^r ↠ F`. This is the
375	unbundled datum whose equivalence classes form the value of the Grassmannian functor. -/
376	structure RankQuotient (r d : ℕ) (T : Scheme.{0}) where
377	  /-- The quotient sheaf. -/
378	  F : T.Modules
379	  /-- The quotient map out of the trivial rank-`r` bundle. -/
380	  q : SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) (Fin r) ⟶ F
381	  /-- The quotient map is an epimorphism (surjective). -/
382	  epi : Epi q
383	  /-- The quotient sheaf is locally free of rank `d`. -/
384	  locFree : SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank F d
385	
386	/-- Two rank-`d` quotients are **equivalent** when there is an isomorphism of the targets
387	commuting with the quotient maps (equivalently, when the kernels of the quotient maps
388	coincide). -/
389	def RankQuotient.Rel {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} (x y : RankQuotient r d T) : Prop :=
390	  ∃ f : x.F ≅ y.F, x.q ≫ f.hom = y.q
391	
392	lemma RankQuotient.rel_refl {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} (x : RankQuotient r d T) : x.Rel x :=
393	  ⟨Iso.refl _, Category.comp_id _⟩
394	
395	lemma RankQuotient.rel_symm {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} {x y : RankQuotient r d T}
396	    (h : x.Rel y) : y.Rel x := by
397	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
398	  exact ⟨f.symm, by rw [Iso.symm_hom, Iso.comp_inv_eq]; exact hf.symm⟩
399	
400	lemma RankQuotient.rel_trans {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} {x y z : RankQuotient r d T}
401	    (h1 : x.Rel y) (h2 : y.Rel z) : x.Rel z := by
402	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h1; obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h2
403	  -- term-mode (the `T.Modules` def-diamond blocks positional category `rw`)
404	  exact ⟨f ≪≫ g,
405	    (congrArg (x.q ≫ ·) (Iso.trans_hom f g)).trans <|
406	      (Category.assoc x.q f.hom g.hom).symm.trans <|
407	        (congrArg (· ≫ g.hom) hf).trans hg⟩
408	
409	/-- The equivalence-of-quotients setoid on `RankQuotient r d T`. -/
410	instance rqSetoid (r d : ℕ) (T : Scheme.{0}) : Setoid (RankQuotient r d T) where
411	  r := RankQuotient.Rel
412	  iseqv := ⟨RankQuotient.rel_refl, RankQuotient.rel_symm, RankQuotient.rel_trans⟩
413	
414	/-- The **pullback action** on a rank-`d` quotient: pull the target sheaf and quotient map
415	back along `ψ`, re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via `pullbackFreeIso`. The
416	result is again an epimorphism (pullback preserves epis) onto a rank-`d` locally free sheaf
417	(`pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank`). -/
418	noncomputable def rqPullback {r d : ℕ} {T' T : Scheme.{0}} (ψ : T' ⟶ T)
419	    (x : RankQuotient r d T) : RankQuotient r d T' where
420	  F := (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).obj x.F
421	  q := (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q
422	  epi :=
423	    -- fully explicit: the def-diamond on `T.Modules` blocks `Epi`-instance search, so
424	    -- `x.epi` is threaded through `map_epi`/`epi_comp` by hand
425	    @CategoryTheory.epi_comp _ _ _ _ _
426	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv inferInstance
427	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q)
428	      (@CategoryTheory.Functor.map_epi _ _ _ _ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ) inferInstance _ _
429	        x.q x.epi)
430	  locFree := Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank ψ x.locFree
431	
432	/-- The pullback action respects the equivalence relation, hence descends to quotients. -/
433	lemma rqPullback_rel {r d : ℕ} {T' T : Scheme.{0}} (ψ : T' ⟶ T)
434	    {x y : RankQuotient r d T} (h : x.Rel y) :
435	    (rqPullback ψ x).Rel (rqPullback ψ y) := by
436	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
437	  refine ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).mapIso f, ?_⟩
438	  change ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q) ≫
439	      (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map f.hom
440	    = (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map y.q
441	  rw [Category.assoc, ← (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map_comp]
442	  exact congrArg
443	    (fun m => (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map m) hf
444	
445	/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(r,d)` (`def:grassmannian_functor`): the
446	contravariant functor from schemes to sets sending `T` to the set of equivalence classes
447	of rank-`d` locally free quotients `q : O_T^r ↠ F`, acting on morphisms by pullback.
448	
449	The object and morphism assignments are complete; the functoriality laws (`map_id`,
450	`map_comp`) are reduced — via the naturality of the pseudofunctor comparison isomorphisms
451	`pullbackId`/`pullbackComp` of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` — to the free-sheaf coherences
452	`pullbackFreeIso (𝟙 T) = (pullbackId T).app (free _)` and the analogue for a composite,
453	which in turn reduce to the unit-level identity `pullbackObjUnitToUnit (𝟙) =
454	(pullbackId).app unit` (and its composite analogue). These remaining coherences between
455	`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` and Mathlib's `pullbackId`/`pullbackComp` are
456	`whnf`-heavy and left as the sole open obstacle. -/
457	noncomputable def functor (d r : ℕ) : Scheme.{0}ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type 1 where
458	  obj T := Quotient (rqSetoid r d T.unop)
459	  map {X Y} g := TypeCat.ofHom (Quotient.map (rqPullback (r := r) (d := d) g.unop)
460	    (fun _ _ h => rqPullback_rel g.unop h))
461	  map_id X := by
462	    -- reduce to the equivalence relation on representatives
463	    ext z
464	    induction z using Quotient.ind with

\section{The functor of points}
\label{sec:grquot_functor}

The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
identities that make that action functorial.

\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
  functor's value at \(T\).

  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
  \[
    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
  \]
  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
  map of equivalence classes.
\end{definition}

The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.

\begin{lemma}[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The pentagon coherence of the
  iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation well-defined.
\end{proof}

Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
functoriality laws (\(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\)) of the Grassmannian
functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.

\begin{definition}
